what_manipulated: evaluation context (separate vs. joint presentation of options)
what_measured: preference rating for a lower-value vs. higher-value option
experiment: Units evaluated two options (gift, ice cream serving, or dinnerware set) differing in quantity and quality. In separate evaluation, units rated each option independently; in joint evaluation, units compared them side-by-side. Outcomes were preference judgments on a continuous scale. Comparison was between-subjects (evaluation context). Statistical tests compared preference ratings across context conditions.

what_manipulated: target role (agent vs. patient in moral scenarios)
experiment: Across seven experiments, units read scenarios or descriptions of targets in different roles (perpetrators, victims, helpers, recipients). Units rated targets on scales assessing moral agency (capacity to act) and moral patiency (capacity to be acted upon). Analysis compared agency and patiency ratings across role types and target categories using within-subjects comparisons and correlation analysis.

what_manipulated: moral threat (unethical behavior prompt vs. control)
what_measured: desire or action toward cleansing (concept accessibility, product preference, behavior)
experiment: Across three experiments, units completed a priming or behavior task introducing moral threat (writing about unethical behavior). Outcomes measured mental accessibility of cleansing concepts (word completion), preference for cleaning products, and likelihood of accepting antiseptic wipes. A fourth outcome measured whether physical cleansing reduced reported distress from moral threat. Units were compared between threat and control conditions.

what_manipulated: behavior framing (whether an action includes intended side effects)
experiment: Units read scenarios describing an agent performing an action with various consequences and rated or categorized whether the behavior was 'intentional.' Outcomes were judgments collected across multiple behavioral scenarios. Analysis examined when units applied the word 'intentional' to actions with foreseen but undesired side effects versus primary intended effects.

what_manipulated: power priming (high vs. low power activation)
experiment: Across four between-subjects experiments and one correlational study, units received power priming (high or low). Outcomes included writing direction (self-oriented E test), knowledge accounting (adjusting for others' information), emotion recognition accuracy, and self-reported perspective-taking tendency. Analysis compared perspective-taking metrics across power conditions and correlated dispositional power with perspective-taking measures.

what_manipulated: physical weight (holding heavy vs. light object)
experiment: Across four experiments, units made importance judgments (Study 1: monetary value; Study 2: fairness of procedure; Studies 3-4: consistency or polarization of related judgments) while holding either a heavy or light clipboard. Outcomes were rating scales, consistency measures across judgments, and argument evaluation. Analysis compared importance-related outcomes between heavy and light object conditions.

what_manipulated: trait activation (thinking about communion vs. agency traits)
experiment: Across three experiments, units thought about or rated personality traits emphasizing either communion (warmth, sociability) or agency dimensions. Outcomes were reported perceptions of ambient room temperature. Analysis compared temperature perceptions between communion and agency conditions, and tested whether effects depended on trait subcategory (sociability vs. morality) or observer versus self-reference.

what_manipulated: valence of past self-representation (self-esteem threat vs. affirmation)
experiment: Across three experiments, units recalled or were assigned past selves or experiences varying in valence (unfavorable versus favorable implications for current self-view). Outcomes were ratings of psychological distance from those past selves. Analysis examined the interaction between unit self-esteem level and past-event valence on subjective distancing, controlling for recall frequency and ease.

what_manipulated: moral credential establishment (expressing diversity-supporting choice vs. control)
what_measured: willingness to express stereotypical or prejudiced opinion
experiment: Across three experiments, units first performed a choice task establishing credentials as non-prejudiced (selecting women or minority candidates) or control. Subsequently, units indicated preference between candidates or attitudes toward social issues where stereotyping might apply. Outcomes were hiring or attitude judgments. Analysis compared prejudiced expression between credential and control conditions.

what_manipulated: mortality salience (mortality prime vs. control; distraction vs. thought engagement)
experiment: Across four studies, units received mortality salience induction or control. In some conditions, units were distracted before bias measurement; in others, they engaged with mortality thoughts or non-death threats. Outcomes were preference ratings for ingroup versus outgroup targets. Analysis examined main effects of mortality salience and moderation by distraction level and thought accessibility of death-related concepts.

what_manipulated: intention to control stereotypes and cognitive resource availability
experiment: Across four experiments, units received stereotype primes (gender-related or neutral words) followed by targets requiring judgment under varying cognitive load and with varying counterstereotype intentions. Outcomes were reaction times and categorization accuracy. Analysis examined stereotype priming effects under conditions of high/low cognitive constraint and with/without counterstereotype intentions.

what_manipulated: gender-related prime exposure and judgment task relevance
what_measured: stereotype-consistent response times and categorization accuracy
experiment: Across two experiments, units viewed primes related to gender or neutral followed by target pronouns (stimulus onset asynchrony 300 ms). In Experiment 1, units judged whether pronouns were male or female; in Experiment 2, units made a gender-irrelevant judgment (pronoun/non-pronoun categorization). Outcomes were reaction times and accuracy. Analysis examined whether automatic stereotyping occurred independent of judgment task relevance and unit stereotype awareness.

what_manipulated: moral self-concept priming (positive vs. negative trait activation)
experiment: In Experiment 1, units wrote self-relevant stories with positive or negative trait words, then indicated willingness to donate. In Experiment 2, units completed similar self-concept priming and outcomes were self-concept change measures. In Experiment 3, units underwent self-concept priming and subsequently made choices in environmental cooperation games. Analysis compared helping/cooperation outcomes between positive and negative self-concept conditions.

what_manipulated: superstition activation (superstition phrase, action, or lucky charm vs. control)
experiment: Across four experiments, units activated superstitions (saying 'break a leg,' crossing fingers, or carrying a charm) or control before performance tasks (golfing, motor dexterity, memory, anagrams). Outcomes were performance accuracy/success and self-efficacy ratings. Analysis compared performance between superstition and control conditions and tested mediation by self-efficacy and task persistence.

what_manipulated: physical temperature experience (holding hot vs. cold object)
what_measured: interpersonal warmth judgment and prosocial choice
experiment: In Study 1, units held a hot or cold coffee cup and rated a target's personality warmth. In Study 2, units held a hot or cold therapeutic pad and chose between a gift for self versus friend. Outcomes were personality warmth ratings and gift-choice behavior. Analysis compared interpersonal warmth outcomes between hot and cold conditions without explicit unit awareness of the temperature-warmth connection.

what_manipulated: music accompaniment during product exposure (liked vs. disliked vs. no music)
experiment: In Experiment 1, units heard liked or disliked music paired with product exposure, then indicated product preference. In Experiment 2, units completed similar music-product pairing in different communication contexts. Outcomes were preference ratings. Analysis examined whether music valence directly affected product preferences via classical conditioning or indirectly through information processing.

what_manipulated: relationship commitment level (priming or measurement)
experiment: A priming experiment activated commitment mindset; a cross-sectional survey measured trait commitment; an interaction record study had units report daily betrayals and forgiveness. Outcomes were explicit forgiveness measures or vengeful-impulse ratings. Analysis examined associations or causal effects of commitment on forgiveness and tested whether effects were mediated by cognitive reinterpretation of betrayal or emotional reactions.

what_manipulated: facial muscle facilitation (pen-holding position inhibiting or facilitating smile muscles)
experiment: In Study 1, units held a pen in mouth positions either facilitating or inhibiting smile muscles, then viewed humorous cartoons and rated humor intensity. In Study 2, units repeated the procedure and additionally rated cartoon cognitive content. Outcomes were humor affect ratings and cognitive assessments. Analysis compared affective humor response between muscle-facilitation conditions and examined whether facial feedback affected cognitive versus affective components.

what_manipulated: verb aspect (imperfective 'was doing' vs. perfective 'did')
experiment: Across three experiments, units read behavioral descriptions using either imperfective or perfective verb aspect and rated the agent's intentionality. Descriptions included mundane and criminal behaviors. Outcomes were intentionality ratings. Analysis examined the effect of aspect on intention attribution and tested mediation by detail of imagined actions.

what_manipulated: pharmacological intervention (methylphenidate vs. placebo)
experiment: Units received methylphenidate or placebo in a double-blind design. After exerting self-control (impulse-regulation task), units performed a subsequent regulatory task (time-on-task or persistence measure). Outcomes were second-task performance and spectral analysis of reaction-time variability. Analysis compared regulatory capacity between drug and placebo conditions and examined frequency-band specificity.
